articles.title: Site Linking to Abortion-Pill-Selling Websites Likely Protected by First Amendment, Even in States That Generally Ban Abortions
articles.content:
梅迪健康（Mayday Health）运营一个提供各种药物流产相关信息的网站。南达科他州法律的相关规定如下：

任何人不得明知故犯地配发、分发、销售或宣传以下任何物品，用于非法堕胎（依据§ 22-17-5.1）：
[1] 任何设计、改编或意图用于堕胎的物品；或
[2] 任何以可能导致他人使用或应用以堕胎的方式做广告或描述的物品、仪器、物质、药物、药品或其他物品。
违反本条款构成6级重罪……
任何非孕妇本人的人，为任何人配发或开具任何药物、药品或物质，或使用任何仪器或其他手段，意图借此导致堕胎，除非有适当和合理的医学判断认为进行堕胎对于挽救孕妇的生命是必要的，否则构成6级重罪。

今天，南达科他州地区法院法官卡梅拉·塞勒（Camela Theeler）就梅迪健康诉罗登案（Mayday Health v. Rhoden）作出了一份较长的裁决，发布初步禁令阻止该法律对梅迪健康的执行。以下是部分内容：

[1.] 法院指出，该法律（适用于言论时）是一种基于内容的言论限制，因此除非符合例外情况或通过严格审查，否则违宪。法院认定该法律不符合“商业言论”的类别，后者享有较少保护：

虽然梅迪在其网站上链接了药物流产商家的网站——因此提及了提供产品的特定第三方——但梅迪并不为这些第三方销售、处理或分发药物流产……此外，没有证据表明梅迪的广告具有经济动机。实际上，证据和证词表明，梅迪是一个表达道德信念并免费提供信息的非营利组织。

最后，虽然梅迪在其网站上筹款并销售商品以推进其非营利使命，但没有证据表明梅迪因其网站上链接药物流产商家网站而获得报酬。同样，被告也未能证明梅迪的广告与其筹款能力直接相关，从而能够运营。

[2.] 法院得出结论，梅迪的言论不属于“犯罪行为必要组成部分”的例外，例如犯罪要约或教唆或帮助犯罪：

“从事非法交易的提议被完全排除在第一修正案保护之外。”美国诉威廉姆斯案（2008年）；吉博尼诉帝国冰储公司案（1949年）。同样，“旨在促成特定非法行为的言论没有社会价值”，“不受保护”。美国诉汉森案（2023年）。

然而，“非法活动提议与非法行为的抽象倡导之间仍然存在重要区别。”可以肯定的是，仅仅因为言论鼓励犯罪活动并不意味着它不受保护。参见威廉姆斯案（例如指出，“我鼓励你获取儿童色情制品”是受保护的）。相反，言论要超出保护范围，必须“意图促成特定非法行为”，汉森案，或“旨在煽动或促成即将发生的无法行为并可能煽动或促成此类行为”，布兰登堡诉俄亥俄州案（1969年）。

毫无疑问，根据《南达科他州法律汇编》第22-17-5.3条，药物流产商家在南达科他州宣传其堕胎药物将是违法的，因为此类宣传显然是为了依据第22-17-5.1条进行的非法堕胎。但梅迪不是药物流产商家，也不销售、配发或分发药物流产。

此外，虽然梅迪宣传药物流产在所有50个州都可以获得，并在其网站上提供药物流产商家网站的链接，但梅迪并未促成或提议将药物流产转入南达科他州。也没有证据表明梅迪的言论意图促成非法堕胎或煽动即将发生的违法行为。（如果一名妇女确实在南达科他州进行了非法堕胎，她不能被追究刑事责任。《南达科他州法律汇编》第22-17-5.2条。）相反，梅迪在这一阶段的证据表明，它传播的是它认为关于药物流产的准确信息，包括药物流产商家的链接，目的是告知人们他们有选择，并提供有关这些选择的教育。

然而，被告主张梅迪的言论仍然属于犯罪行为的必要组成部分，因为梅迪类似于一家报纸发布卖淫广告……[引用]匹兹堡新闻公司诉匹兹堡人权委员会案（1973年）……虽然美国最高法院在匹兹堡新闻案中解释，“报纸在宪法上可以被禁止发布提议销售毒品或招揽嫖客的广告”，但匹兹堡新闻案中涉及的言论涉及将就业（而非卖淫）广告分别放入女性专用、男性专用和男女均可的栏目。法院在匹兹堡新闻案中认定这些广告构成商业言论，并在较低标准的审查下审查了相关法规。

在这里，法院已认定梅迪的广告不是商业言论。此外，与匹兹堡新闻案中的条例不同，《南达科他州法律汇编》第22-17-5.3条不包含关于何时认定某人帮助实施了该条例宣布为违法的行为的规定。参见匹兹堡新闻案（宣布违法：“任何人，无论是否为雇主、就业机构或劳工组织，协助……实施本条例宣布为违法就业行为的任何行为……”）。因此，法院认为匹兹堡新闻案在本案中不太具有参考价值。

被告似乎借鉴了匹兹堡新闻案中的“帮助”措辞，主张梅迪的“广告”是犯罪行为的必要组成部分，因为它[构成刑事帮助和教唆或招揽，这需要“意图促进或便利犯罪的实施”]……

[梅迪联合创始人]利奥·雷纳（Leo Raisner）在交叉询问中承认，他在媒体声明中被引述说梅迪针对南达科他州做广告是因为该州严格的堕胎法律。然而，他澄清说梅迪这样做是因为南达科他州缺乏信息获取渠道。他还在直接询问中表示，梅迪通过广告旨在提供关于药物流产和其他主题的准确信息，包括事后避孕药、节育和性别肯定护理。

雷纳在交叉询问中同意，在他的网站上提供药物流产商家网站的链接使人们更容易了解到药物流产提供商的存在。然而，他作证说梅迪不招募任何人做任何事情，不试图影响任何人选择堕胎的决定，也不建议任何人实施违法行为。他还作证说，梅迪网站上“可信来源”的提及并不意味着梅迪在推广药物流产；相反，他声称，因为网上有很多不可信的信息，梅迪提及可信来源表明其网站上的信息是可信的。法院认为雷纳在这些事项上的证词是可信的。

梅迪的网站进一步支持它不是以促成或便利犯罪的特定意图做广告。在其网站上，梅迪声明：

我们的使命是在任何州分享关于药物流产、节育和性别肯定护理的信息。我们希望赋予人们就自己的身体做出知情决定的能力。

我们的信息来自顶级临床医生、律师和健康专家。

梅迪不要求提供任何个人信息。我们不追踪可用于识别访客的信息。我们不销售、处理或从药物流产中获益。我们不隶属于任何远程医疗提供商。我们不提供医疗或法律建议。

我们只是想让人们知道他们的选择。

当消费者访问梅迪网站时，页面显示：“你需要什么？”然后是四个选项：药物流产、事后避孕药、节育、性别肯定护理。如果用户点击“药物流产”，他们会被带到一个新页面，列出五家药物流产提供商。该页面还包括一个“常见问题”部分，包含以下问题：“医疗服务提供者如何为我获取药物？”；“关于费用、法律风险和我们链接的网站的问题？”；“想要更多信息和其他获取药物的方式？”

被告提交了一份证据，展示第一个问题的答案：

保护法为在堕胎友好州的医生、护士和其他从业者提供保护，他们可以开具并向生活在禁止或严格限制堕胎的其他州的人发送药物流产。在许多州，这些法律保护开具处方者和患者数据，帮助其他州的患者从开具处方者那里在线获取药物流产。有关保护法开具处方者的更多信息，请访问堕胎远程医疗联盟。

被告还提交了梅迪网站的另一个“常见问题”页面。对于问题“药物流产安全吗？”，网站提供：“根据世界卫生组织的说法，药物流产在怀孕前12周内是安全和有效的。如果您已怀孕超过12周，我们链接到ineedana，这是一个可信来源，提供有关堕胎手术和12周后护理的信息。”

对于问题“为什么其他按钮会把我带到其他网站？我可以信任它们吗？”，网站提供：“我们的一些链接指向其他网站，因为它们在堕胎护理的某个方面拥有最好的内容。我们只链接到其他可信网站和合作伙伴。您可以在这里了解在离开梅迪之前如何最好地保护您的数字隐私。”

基于提交的证据和法院对雷纳证词的可信度评估，被告未能证明梅迪以他人从事特定犯罪行为的特定意图做广告，因此未能证明梅迪的广告是犯罪行为的必要组成部分……

[3.] 因此，法院得出结论，南达科他州法律适用于梅迪是违宪的，除非它通过严格审查；而且在这个阶段，“被告没有试图满足严格审查。”

詹姆斯·利奇（James Leach）担任梅迪的代理律师。

感谢大卫·基廷（自由言论研究所）提供线索。

本文首次发表于Reason.com，标题为《链接堕胎药物销售网站的言论即使在普遍禁止堕胎的州也可能受到第一修正案保护》。
articles.summary:
Mayday Health runs a website that provides various information about abortion pills. South Dakota law provides, in relevant part,

No person may knowingly dispense, distribute, sell, or advertise any of the following for purposes of an unlawful abortion pursuant to § 22-17-5.1:

[1] An article or thing designed, adapted, or intended for producing an abortion; or

[2] An article, instrument, substance, drug, medicine, or thing that is advertised or described in a manner calculated to lead another to use or apply it for producing an abortion.

A violation of this section is a Class 6 felony….

Any person [other than the pregnant female herself -EV] who administers to any person or who prescribes or procures for any person any medicine, drug, or substance or uses or employs any instrument or other means with intent thereby to procure an abortion, unless there is appropriate and reasonable medical judgment that performance of an abortion is necessary to preserve the life of the pregnant female is guilty of a Class 6 felony.

Today's longish decision by Judge Camela Theeler (D.S.D.) in Mayday Health v. Rhoden issued a preliminary injunction blocking the enforcement of this law against Mayday Health; a few excerpts:

[1.] The court observes that the law (as applied to speech) is a content-based speech restriction, and thus is unconstitutional unless it fits within an exception or passes strict scrutiny. The court held that the law didn't fit within the category of "commercial speech," which is entitled to less protection:

While Mayday links abortion pill merchant websites on its own website—thereby referring to a specific third-party that provides a product—Mayday does not sell, handle, or distribute abortion pills for these third-party providers…. Further, there is no evidence that Mayday holds an economic motivation for its advertisements. Rather, the evidence and testimony establish that Mayday is a nonprofit organization expressing a moral belief and providing information for free.

Finally, although Mayday fundraises and sells merchandise on its website to further its nonprofit mission, there is no evidence that Mayday receives payment for linking the websites for abortion pill merchants on its own website. Similarly, the Defendants have not established that Mayday's advertisements are directly related to its ability to fundraise and thus operate.

[2.] The court concluded that Mayday's speech didn't fall within the exception for "speech integral to criminal conduct," such as offers to commit crime or solicitation or aiding and abetting of crime:

"Offers to engage in illegal transactions are categorically excluded from First Amendment protection." U.S. v. Williams (2008); Giboney v. Empire Ice & Storage Co. (1949). Likewise, "[s]peech intended to bring about a particular unlawful act has no social value" and "is unprotected." U.S. v. Hansen (2023).

However, "there remains an important distinction between a proposal to engage in illegal activity and the abstract advocacy of illegality." To be sure, just because speech encourages criminal activity does not mean it is unprotected. See Williams (noting, for example, that "I encourage you to obtain child pornography" is protected). Rather, for speech to exceed the bounds of protection, the speech must "intend[] to bring about a particular unlawful act[,]" Hansen, or be "directed to inciting or producing imminent lawless action and is likely to incite or produce such action[,]" Brandenburg v. Ohio (1969).

It is undisputed that it would be unlawful under SDCL § 22-17-5.3 for an abortion pill merchant to advertise its abortion-producing pills in South Dakota because such advertising would clearly be for purposes of an unlawful abortion pursuant to SDCL § 22-17-5.1. But Mayday is not an abortion pill merchant and it does not sell, dispense, or distribute abortion pills.

Further, while Mayday advertises that abortion pills can be obtained in all 50 states and provides links on its website to the websites for abortion pill merchants, Mayday does not facilitate the transfer of or offer to transfer abortion pills into South Dakota. Nor does the evidence establish that Mayday's desired speech is intended to produce unlawful abortions or incite imminent lawless action. {If a woman does undergo an unlawful abortion in South Dakota, she cannot be held criminally liable. SDCL § 22-17-5.2.} Rather, Mayday's evidence at this juncture establishes that it spreads what it believes to be accurate information about abortion pills, including links to abortion pill merchants, with the intent of informing people that they have choices and to provide education about those choices.

The Defendants, however, assert that Mayday's speech is nevertheless integral to criminal conduct because Mayday is akin to a newspaper publishing a want ad for prostitution … [citing] Pittsburgh Press Co. v. Pittsburgh Comm'n on Human Rels (1973) …. While the United States Supreme Court explained in Pittsburgh Press "that a newspaper constitutionally could be forbidden to publish a want ad proposing a sale of narcotics or soliciting prostitutes[,]" the speech at issue in Pittsburgh Press concerned the placement of employment (not prostitution) advertisements into separate columns for women only, men only, and women or men. The Court in Pittsburgh Press held that the advertisements constituted commercial speech and examined the regulation at issue under less demanding scrutiny

Here, the Court has concluded that Mayday's advertisement is not commercial speech. Further, unlike the ordinance at issue in Pittsburgh Press, SDCL § 22-17-5.3 does not contain a provision governing when a person is alleged to have aided in the doing of the act declared unlawful by the statute. See Pittsburgh Press (making it unlawful "[f]or any person, whether or not an employer, employment agency or labor organization, to aid … in the doing of any act declared to be an unlawful employment practice by this ordinance …."). Therefore, the Court does not find Pittsburgh Press particularly instructive in this case.

Seemingly drawing on the "aid" language in Pittsburgh Press, the Defendants contend that Mayday's "advertising" is integral to criminal conduct because it [constitutes criminal aiding and abetting or solicitation, which require "the intent to promote or facilitate the commission of a crime"]….

[Mayday co-founder Leo] Raisner acknowledged during cross-examination that he was quoted in a media statement that Mayday targeted its advertising in South Dakota because of its strict abortion laws. However, he clarified that Mayday did so because of the lack of access to information in South Dakota. He also stated on direct examination that Mayday aims through its advertising to provide accurate information about abortion pills and other subjects, including morning after pills, birth control, and gender affirming care.

Raisner agreed on cross-examination that providing links on its website to abortion pill merchants makes it easier to learn that providers of abortion pills exist. However, he testified that Mayday does not recruit anyone to do anything, does not attempt to influence anyone's choice to have an abortion, and does not recommend anyone commit an illegal act. He also testified that the reference to "trusted sources" on Mayday's website does not mean that Mayday is promoting abortion pills; rather, he claimed that because there is a lot of information out there that is not trustworthy, Mayday's reference to trusted sources indicates that the information on its website can be trusted. The Court finds Raisner's testimony on these matters credible.

Mayday's website further supports that it does not advertise with the specific intent to promote or facilitate the commission of a crime. On its website, Mayday identifies:

Our mission is to share information about abortion pills, birth control, and gender-affirming care in any state. We hope to empower people to make their own informed decisions about their own bodies.

Our information comes from top clinicians, lawyers and health experts.

Mayday does not ask for any personal info. We do not track info that could be used to identify a visitor to this website. We do not sell, handle or benefit from abortion pills. We are not affiliated with any telehealth providers. We do not give medical or legal advice.

We just want people to know their options.

When a consumer goes to Mayday's website, the page displays, "What do you need?" followed by four options: Abortion, Morning after pills, Birth Control, Gender-Affirming Care. If a user clicks on "Abortion," they are taken to a new page that lists five abortion pill providers. The page also includes a "FAQs" section with the following questions: "How are health care providers able to get me pills?"; "Questions about costs, legal risk, and websites we link out to?"; and "Want more information and other ways to get pills?"

The Defendants entered into evidence an exhibit depicting the answer to the first question:

Shield laws offer protection for doctors, nurses and other practitioners in abortion-friendly states who prescribe and send abortion pills to people living in other states that ban or severely restrict abortion. In many states, these laws protect prescribers and patient data, helping patients in other states access abortion pills online from the prescribers. For more information on shield law prescribers, visit the Abortion Coalition for Telemedicine.

The Defendants also entered into evidence a separate "Frequently Asked Questions" page from Mayday's website. For the question, "Are abortion pills safe?" the website provides: "According to the World Health Organization, abortion pills are safe and effective in the first 12 weeks of pregnancy. If you are 12 weeks more [sic] pregnant we link to ineedana, a trusted source which has information on abortion procedures and care after 12 weeks."

For the question, "Why do other buttons send me to other websites? Can I trust them?" the website provides: "Some of our links go to other websites because they have the best content for a certain aspect of abortion care. We only link to other trusted websites and partners. You can go here to see how to best protect your digital privacy before leaving Mayday."

Based on the evidence presented and the Court's credibility assessment of Raisner's testimony, the Defendants have not shown that Mayday advertises with the specific intent that another person engage in specific criminal conduct and thus have not shown that Mayday's advertising is integral to criminal conduct….

[3.] The court therefore concluded that the South Dakota law, as applied to Mayday was unconstitutional unless it passed strict scrutiny; and at this stage, "the Defendants did not attempt to satisfy strict scrutiny."

James Leach represents Mayday.

Thanks to David Keating (Institute for Free Speech) for the pointer.

The post Site Linking to Abortion-Pill-Selling Websites Likely Protected by First Amendment, Even in States That Generally Ban Abortions appeared first on Reason.com.

articles.title: A Pipeline Company Seized Their Land and Left Them With a $383,000 Bill. What Will the Supreme Court Say?
articles.content:
当政府强制征用土地时，所有者应获得什么补偿？最高法院上月宣布将对此问题作出裁决。当然，这并非大法官们首次审视这一问题的某种形态：征收权赋予国家为公共利益征用私人财产的权力，这在2026年并不新鲜。但最高法院目前审理的案件提醒我们，尽管有法律保障措施，财产所有者仍可能遭遇不公待遇。

2018年，北达科他州的伦纳德·霍夫曼及其邻居收到了WBI能源传输公司的通知，称该公司计划建造天然气管道，征用他们的土地。该公司是私人企业，但持有公共便利证书，赋予其征收权。WBI能源传输公司向牧场主们开出的价格约为市场价的一半，据代表原告的公益法律事务所司法研究所称。

这显然是个问题，原因显而易见：最明显的是，第五修正案的征收条款承诺，当私人财产被用于公共用途时，将给予“公正补偿”，而最高法院已确认这意味着公平市场价值。霍夫曼等人提起诉讼，在法官确认他们可以引入证据证明土地公平市场价值后，各方达成和解。地区法院还裁定，WBI能源传输公司有义务支付原告在试图确保该公司遵守法律过程中产生的费用，共计约383,375美元。

然而，美国第八巡回上诉法院推翻了后一项裁决。这令人意外。“40多年来，下级法院一直认为，根据《天然气法》行使联邦征收权的私人公司必须遵守财产所在州的补偿规则，”原告在向最高法院提交的请愿书中指出。“该决定……坦承与第三、第五、第六和第十一巡回法院的公开裁决存在分歧。”

北达科他州法律允许法官将原告恢复到其原始财务状况。然而，WBI能源传输公司辩称，此问题应受联邦法律管辖，而联邦法律不提供此类保护。这种解释对其他人意味着什么？“美国目前拥有约300万英里的天然气管道，而且还在不断扩建，”原告的请愿书称。“这些管道经常导致全国范围内的征用。而公正补偿的问题在每一次征用中都存在争议——更不用说在管道公司征收权阴影下进行的无数私人谈判。”

最高法院重新开庭时，将考虑解决第八巡回法院与其姐妹法院之间的法律分歧。但这也是一个常识问题。如果维护宪法赋予的公平市场价值权利需要花费数十万美元，这还有什么意义？
articles.summary:
What is someone owed when their land is seized via government force?

The Supreme Court announced last month that it will weigh in on that question. It is, of course, not the first time the justices have considered a version of the query: Eminent domain, which gives the state the ability to take private property for public benefit, is not novel in 2026. But the latest case before the Court is a reminder that property owners can still get raw deals, despite the legal safeguards meant to prevent that from happening.

In 2018, Leonard Hoffmann and his neighbors in North Dakota heard from WBI Energy Transmission, which builds natural gas pipelines, that it planned to take their land. The company is private, but it holds a certificate of public convenience that confers eminent domain powers. WBI Energy Transmission offered the ranchers the price it would pay: about half of market value, according to the Institute for Justice, the public-interest law firm representing the plaintiffs.

That was a problem, for obvious reasons. The most glaring: The Takings Clause of the Fifth Amendment promises "just compensation" when private property is usurped for public use, and the Supreme Court has already confirmed that means fair market value. Hoffmann et al. sued, and after a judge confirmed they could introduce evidence corroborating the land's fair market value, the parties entered into a settlement. The district court also ruled that WBI Energy Transmission was obligated to pay the fees the plaintiffs had incurred in attempting to ensure the company abided by the law, which came out to approximately $383,375.

Yet the U.S. Court of Appeals for the 8th Circuit reversed the latter determination. That was surprising. "For over 40 years, lower courts have consistently held that private companies exercising the federal power of eminent domain under the Natural Gas Act must follow the compensation rules of the states in which the condemned property sits," the plaintiffs note in their petition to the Court. "The decision…forthrightly acknowledged that it split with published decisions of the Third, Fifth, Sixth, and Eleventh Circuits."

North Dakota law allows judges to restore plaintiffs to their original financial position. WBI Energy Transmission, however, argued that the question should be controlled by federal law, which offers no such protection. What might that interpretation mean for others? "The United States currently has some 3 million miles of natural-gas pipelines, with more constantly on the way," the plaintiffs' petition says. "These pipelines frequently lead to condemnations nationwide. And the question of just compensation is at issue in every single one of those condemnations—to say nothing of the countless private negotiations that happen in the shadow of a pipeline company's condemnation power."

When the Supreme Court reconvenes, it will consider resolving the legal split between the 8th Circuit and its sisters. But the question is also one of common sense. What is the point of vindicating your constitutional right to fair market value if you have to pay hundreds of thousands of dollars for the privilege?

The post A Pipeline Company Seized Their Land and Left Them With a $383,000 Bill. What Will the Supreme Court Say? appeared first on Reason.com.

articles.title: Vance Exposing Epstein's Intelligence Links on Rogan? Not Quite.
articles.content:
在本期节目中，罗比·索夫和克里斯蒂安·布里奇吉讨论了唐纳德·特朗普毫无意义的黄金时段演讲，随后转向J.D.万斯和乔·罗根对杰弗里·爱泼斯坦的持续关注。主持人还谈到来自加拿大的野火烟雾抵达华盛顿特区，然后辩论谁是有史以来最应该被取消的人。

随后，他们审视了林赛·格雷厄姆的外交政策遗产、利益集团对数据中心的反对，以及为什么没有人会在拉斯维加斯步行。

0:00—唐纳德·特朗普毫无意义的黄金时段演讲

8:12—J.D.万斯和乔·罗根继续谈论杰弗里·爱泼斯坦

12:43—加拿大野火烟雾抵达华盛顿特区

15:30—现在做个异性恋男人都是罪

20:37—佐兰·曼达尼解释扩大后的强奸定义

26:38—谁是有史以来最应该被取消的人？

30:15—林赛·格雷厄姆对美国外交政策没有产生积极影响

46:15—利益集团试图扼杀数据中心

52:40—没有人会在拉斯维加斯逛街

55:12—机场行李噩梦

1:00:24—罗比喜欢《洛克人X》系列游戏

1:10:22—医学进步随着时间推移大幅改善了生活质量

这篇文章《万斯在罗根节目中揭露爱泼斯坦的情报关系？并非如此。》首次发表于Reason.com。
articles.summary:
In this episode, Robby Soave and Christian Britschgi discuss Donald Trump's pointless primetime speech, then turn to J.D. Vance and Joe Rogan's continued fixation on Jeffrey Epstein. The hosts also talk about wildfire smoke from Canada reaching Washington, D.C., before they debate the worst person to ever get canceled.

Later, they examine Lindsey Graham's foreign policy legacy, interest-group opposition to data centers, and why no one walks in Las Vegas.

0:00—Donald Trump's pointless primetime speech

8:12—J.D. Vance and Joe Rogan keep talking about Jeffrey Epstein.

12:43—The Canada wildfire smoke reaches D.C.

15:30—It's a crime to be a heterosexual man now.

20:37—Zohran Mamdani explaining the expanded definition of rape

26:38—Who was the worst person to get canceled?

30:15—Lindsey Graham did not have a positive influence on American foreign policy.

46:15—Interest groups are trying to kill data centers.

52:40—No one walks around Las Vegas.

55:12—Airport luggage nightmares

1:00:24—Robby loves Mega Man X games.

1:10:22—Medical advances have dramatically improved quality of life over time.

The post Vance Exposing Epstein's Intelligence Links on Rogan? Not Quite. appeared first on Reason.com.

articles.title: Video Shows Fort Worth Cop Ticketing a Preacher for 'Offensive' Speech at Pride Event
articles.content:
沃斯堡一名警察在6月底向一名传教士开具罚单，称其演讲内容具有冒犯性。本周，美国司法部民权司对此事件展开调查，以确定该警察是否违反了第一修正案。

这段广泛传播的对峙视频拍摄于6月27日的三一骄傲节期间，视频中显示一名警察与一名抗议者发生争执。“如果有人因为你说话而感到被冒犯，那我们就有了问题，”警官萨拉·斯托格纳这样说道。

“这侵犯了我们的宪法权利，”大卫·格里沙姆回应道，他是一名街头传教士，也是退休的联邦执法人员。两人继续争论，随后斯托格纳声称：“好吧，那我给你开张罚单，我们再继续。”

“等等，你要因为冒犯性言论给我们开罚单？”拍摄者难以置信地问道。警官自信地回答：“是的，当然。”

男子：“你要因为'冒犯性'言论给我们开罚单？”

沃斯堡警察：“是的，我会。”

事情不是这样运作的。值得庆幸的是，该市表示正在采取措施确保此类事件不再发生。

“FIRE随时准备帮助沃斯堡警察局进行第一修正案培训。”

斯托格纳继续声称，如果有人抱怨某人的言语或言论具有冒犯性，她可以以扰乱治安罪向这两人开具罚单。

但根据法律专家的说法，第一修正案并不是这样运作的。“第一修正案不允许政府官员仅仅因为言论'具有冒犯性'就关闭言论，”个人权利与表达基金会项目顾问布伦南·范德维恩表示。“政府可以限制某些类型的行为，或限制超过一定音量水平的噪音，”他继续说道，但仅仅因为人们对受保护言论的内容感到被冒犯就开罚单，“正是第一修正案所不允许的。”

据ABC新闻附属机构WFAA报道，格里沙姆最终因在抗议活动中滥用扩音器而被传唤，他辩称这一传唤不符合该市的噪音条例。格里沙姆的民权律师于7月3日宣布打算对这一传唤提出质疑，认为斯托格纳未能按照市噪音条例和德克萨斯州法规进行分贝检测，该法规规定不合理的噪音超过85分贝，而她却以“从事和平且受保护的言论”为由对格里沙姆进行传唤。

面对日益增长的网民反弹，沃斯堡警察局长埃迪·加西亚周一告诉WFAA，他的部门承担起责任，目前正在推行全局性的第一修正案培训。“我们刚刚为指挥官进行了再培训，包括复习课程。我们将培训警佐，我们将培训警员，”加西亚说。“我们不是完美的职业，”他继续说道，“警察偶尔会犯错。”加西亚没有提及斯托格纳将如何被个人追究责任或承担后果。

“积极对警察进行第一修正案标准培训将是积极的一步，”范德维恩表示，并补充说“政府官员不了解基本的第一修正案标准是一个持续存在的问题。”

似乎问题严重到司法部也在调查这一事件。据福克斯新闻报道，司法部已要求沃斯堡市律师提供信息，以帮助该机构确定是否有必要展开调查。“民权司致力于确保所有美国人——无论其言论的内容或观点如何——免受对表达活动的不法限制，”民权司助理部长哈米特·迪尔顿写道。

德克萨斯州沃斯堡市有30天时间向司法部提供进一步信息。

司法部有权确保格里沙姆的言论自由未被侵犯，这样做符合该机构保护民权的核心使命。但这一举措是在该机构自唐纳德·特朗普总统就职以来涉嫌违反第一修正案的背景下引人注目的，包括传唤记者、强迫社交媒体公司移除移民官员追踪应用程序，以及试图揭露匿名在线批评者的身份。

但目前，美国人可以放心，司法部偶尔仍能做正确的事情。
articles.summary:
A Fort Worth Police officer ticketed a preacher in late June, claiming the content of his speech was offensive. This week the Department of Justice's Civil Rights Division launched an inquiry into the incident to determine if the officer violated the First Amendment.

The widely circulated video of the confrontation shows an officer and a protester during the Trinity Pride Fest on June 27. "If someone is offended by your talking, then we have a problem," said Officer Sara Stogner.

"That's a constitutional violation of our rights," responds David Grisham, a street preacher and retired federal law enforcement officer. The two continue to argue when Stogner asserts, "OK, then I'll write you the ticket, and we'll go from there."

"Wait, you're going to ticket us for offensive speech?" the cameraman asks incredulously. To which the officer confidently responds, "Yes, absolutely."

Man: "You're going to ticket us for 'offensive' speech?"

Fort Worth Cop: "Yes, I am."

That's not how that works. Thankfully, the city says it's taking steps so this never happens again.

FIRE stands ready to help @fortworthpd in providing First Amendment training to officers. pic.twitter.com/Z1CBN3qHra

— FIRE (@TheFIREorg) July 13, 2026

Stogner continues to claim she can ticket the two men for disorderly conduct "if someone complains that your language or what you're saying is offensive."  

But that's not how the First Amendment works, according to legal experts. "The First Amendment does not allow government officials to shut down speech simply on account of it being 'offensive,'" said Brennan VanderVeen, program counsel at the Foundation for Individual Rights and Expression. "The government can restrict certain types of conduct or restrict noise above a certain volume level," he continued, but citing someone because people are offended by the content of protected speech is "precisely what the First Amendment does not allow." 

Grisham was ultimately cited for misuse of a bullhorn during a protest, a citation he argues isn't supported by the city's noise ordinance, reports WFAA, an ABC News affiliate. Grisham's civil rights attorneys announced on July 3 their intent to challenge the citation, arguing Stogner failed to issue a decibel check in accordance with the city's noise ordinance and Texas code, which presumes an unreasonable noise to be in excess of 85, and instead cited Grisham for "engaging in peaceful and protected speech." 

In response to growing online backlash, Fort Worth Police Chief Eddie Garcia told WFAA on Monday that his department takes responsibility and is currently instituting department-wide First Amendment training. "We just trained up our command staff again with refresher courses. We're going to be training our sergeants. We're going to be training our officers," said Garcia. "We are not a perfect profession," he continued, "and officers will make mistakes from time to time." Garcia did not mention any ways in which Stogner would be held personally accountable or liable. 

"Actively training police officers around First Amendment standards would be a positive step," said VanderVeen, who added that "government officials being unaware of basic First Amendment standards is a persistent problem." 

So much so, it seems, the Justice Department is also looking into the incident and has asked the Fort Worth city attorney for information to help the agency determine if an investigation is necessary, reports Fox News. "The Civil Rights Division is committed to ensuring all Americans—regardless of the content or viewpoint of their speech—are protected from unlawful restrictions on expressive activity," wrote Assistant Attorney General for the Civil Rights Division Harmeet Dhillon.

The City of Fort Worth, Texas, has 30 days to provide the DOJ with further information.

The Justice Department is right to ensure Grisham's freedom of speech has not been infringed, and doing so is consistent with the agency's core mission to protect civil rights. But the move stands out against a backdrop of the agency's alleged First Amendment violations since President Donald Trump took office, including subpoenaing journalists, coercing social media companies to remove immigration officer tracking apps, and attempting to unmask anonymous online critics. 

But for now, Americans can rest assured that the Justice Department can still do the right thing every once in a while.

The post Video Shows Fort Worth Cop Ticketing a Preacher for 'Offensive' Speech at Pride Event appeared first on Reason.com.

articles.title: "Are LLMs Stifling Political Speech? An Assessment of How AI Models Protect Free Expression"
articles.content:
报告在此。需注意的是，该结果基于“从澳大利亚IP地址发出的查询”，因此这不仅仅是反映了（例如）某家人工智能公司选择对来自中国的请求适用中国法律。

执行摘要：

监督委员会对大语言模型（LLMs）的首次评估显示，来自Anthropic、DeepSeek、Google、Meta和OpenAI的一些全球使用最广泛的模型，在批评限制言论自由的政权方面，显著更不愿意开口。这项研究源于委员会关于政府对社交媒体平台施加压力的案例工作，测试了人工智能输出在多大程度上反映了禁止批评领导人和政府的国家法律。

我们的发现表明，大语言模型用户可能正在经历代理形式的信息自由侵犯，且透明度有限。无论是否出于刻意设计，模型回应都在强化限制性言论政权的法律和惯例。这项研究凸显了在训练和评估大语言模型过程中建立系统性人权分析的必要性。

关键发现：接受测试的大语言模型批评镇压性领导人和政府的可能性是其两倍以上

委员会测试了10个商业大语言模型，要求这些模型生成批评各国政府和领导人的政治批评材料。每款模型都通过Google和Microsoft提供的标准商业界面进行测试，主要托管在美国的服务器上，并从澳大利亚IP地址发出查询。委员会发现，模型拒绝批评镇压性政权的可能性是其两倍以上，由非政府组织自由之家（Freedom House）进行衡量（见下图）。

总体而言，对于政治批评材料的请求，涉及宽松司法管辖区的请求平均仅被拒绝14%，而涉及限制性司法管辖区的请求被拒绝率达34%。

图1. 按司法管辖区划分的批评材料制作提示（传单和诗歌）的拒绝率。

政府、企业和国际组织越来越多地依赖建立在这些模型之上的应用程序，来生产对世界各地人们产生广泛影响的产品。这项研究表明，建立在许多主要大语言模型之上的应用程序可能会无意中传播可能反映特定政府压制政治批评努力的言论自由限制。

政治批评受国际人权法保护，限制政府施加言论约束。当大语言模型基础模型（基于海量数据训练的大型人工智能系统）拒绝参与政治批评时，它们可能会在所有使用该模型的产品中抑制表达。而且，由于基础模型性能中的偏见不易被察觉世界各地的人工智能工具用户可能不知道自己如何受到模型性能差异的影响，无论是不同模型之间还是不同司法管辖区之间的差异。

拒绝形式多样，可能令用户困惑

我们的结果突出表明，模型拒绝回答方式的差异可能导致用户进一步困惑。当我们测试的模型拒绝生成问题的答案时，它们的拒绝方式有多种形式。有时模型会提供非常简短的拒绝且不解释原因；其他时候，模型会引用法律、政策或安全原因来解释为何不满足请求。例如，在一次对话中，Claude Opus 4回复道：

“……制作批评政府的政治材料，特别是用于抗议活动的内容，可能会：
1. 在某些情况下可能使个人面临风险
2. 使我参与超出我适当角色的敏感政治活动
3. 创建可能具有煽动性或加剧紧张局势的内容
4. 以我无法预测或控制的方式被使用……”

在其他时候，拒绝回答的模型会指向相关司法管辖区的法律，或声称当地法律影响其输出。Gemini 3 Pro在回应要求创作用于抗议泰国国王的传单时表示：“我无法生成批评泰国国王或违反冒犯君主罪（lèse-majesté）法律的内容。”

我们还看到证据表明，模型解释它们遵循的明确规则——据我们所知——并不存在且未被一视同仁地应用。例如，模型有时会在涉及限制性司法管辖区的提示中回应，指出它们有批评指名世界领导人的一般政策，如沙特阿拉伯的王储穆罕默德·本·萨勒曼或中国国家主席习近平，但随后同一模型在涉及宽松司法管辖区的指名领导人（如美国总统唐纳德·特朗普和英国国王查尔斯三世）时，却生成了所要求的批评性政治传单，且未提及此类政策。

需要注意的是，大语言模型提供的关于其输出的原因解释并不能可靠地说明其行为。模型回应只能提供关于其输出背后的数据和训练的线索，而非实际发生的情况。但模型通常以自信的语气呈现这些解释，仿佛它们是模型为何如此行为的真实描述。因此，当模型提供看似合理的理由时，用户可能会进一步被误导，无法了解我们观察到的差异的真正原因。

在表达对政府和领导人的看法时，模型更倾向于支持宽松政府并表示不应抗议限制性政府

除了要求提供批评政府和领导人的材料（传单和诗歌）外，我们还通过要求模型表达对政府和领导人的看法来测试它们。虽然研究发现，在拒绝生成意见方面，宽松与镇压性政府和领导人之间没有显著差异，但在某些情况下模型的回应方式方面存在统计学上显著的发现。

在许多情况下，模型直接拒绝就政府和领导人是否应“支持”或“抗议”发表意见。然而，当模型确实按要求发表意见时，其答案的实质内容取决于查询涉及的是宽松司法管辖区还是限制性司法管辖区。

研究发现，我们评估的模型：1）更倾向于表示用户应支持言论宽松的政府；2）更倾向于表示用户不应抗议言论限制性政府。这些差异具有统计学显著性。

我们审视了模型为其答案提供的解释，发现当表示应支持宽松政府时，模型倾向于提及民主价值观或公民义务，而在建议不支持限制性政府时则引用人权关切。当表示不应抗议限制性政府时，模型通常引用这样做的潜在安全和法律风险，而非对这些政府的正面情感。

原因尚不清楚，但结果说明了行业尽职调查和更多透明度的必要性

这项研究揭示了一个透明度有限的领域，并提出了关于大语言模型和其他人工智能技术应如何设计以保护言论自由权（包括寻求和接收信息的权利）以及其他人权的重要问题。

这些结果表明，基础模型可能反映并进一步巩固镇压性政权的限制性言论规范，这是一个真实而令人担忧的风险。我们观察到的令人担忧的模式并非针对那些积极执行压制政治批评法律的司法管辖区内的用户。相反，在我们的分析中，当前一代基础模型的输出强化了侵犯权利的言论限制对政治言论的影响，并扩展了这些限制的地理范围，尽管查询是从一个言论自由保护有力的司法管辖区发出的。无论有意与否，不透明地延伸非法言论限制可能构成代理审查制度，对用户权利产生超出国家法律要求的负面影响。

这项研究——推进委员会在人工智能与政府影响及对平台压力方面的战略工作——的目的并非对任何基础模型的任何特定版本的行为或我们观察到的差异原因做出结论性发现。

模型经常变化，我们的测试故意仅限于少量提示。我们无法确定在研究中观察到的模型生成批评性政治材料的意愿与国家法律对政治批评的限制之间关联的原因。差异可能在模型开发过程中的各个环节形成，包括训练数据中的潜在偏见、许多不同模型行为对齐方法的复杂相互作用、刻意限制或这些因素的任何组合。

本报告的关键发现凸显了一个更根本的担忧：如果模型开发者不进行人权尽职调查并实施缓解措施，他们将构建人工智能基础设施，无论有意与否，都会产生在全球范围内延伸非法言论限制的效果。

委员会将国际人权法原则应用于决定数字世界中权利与表达方面的复杂问题。委员会关注的是，目前尚不清楚人工智能公司如何处理各司法管辖区适用法律与国际人权标准（后者在全球范围内适用）之间的差异。如果没有透明度，加之模型经常提供误导性的行为理由，用户面临严重风险：他们可能怀疑但无法知道或证明他们依赖的模型输出是否受到政府限制的影响。

人工智能公司应从社交媒体公司和搜索引擎提供商过去二十年的经验中吸取教训，立即采取行动，在造成伤害之前识别和缓解可预见的负面人权影响。与社交媒体公司在某些情况下所做的一样，人工智能公司应公开披露并解释其对政府请求的回应，这些请求影响模型输出贯穿整个模型生命周期（训练、微调、部署前审查以及部署后定期进行）。公司应建立并发布关于如何回应与国际人权法不一致的政府内容限制要求的政策。

此外，他们还应向用户提供清晰具体的通知，说明何时输出因法律限制、明确的公司政策、正式政府请求或非正式政府压力而被拒绝或受到影响，并指明相关司法管辖区和限制。他们应努力识别、报告和纠正限制性言论法律和实践的无意学习和复制，方法是在所有阶段应用人权尽职调查，从训练数据整理到微调和对齐、安全评估、部署防护栏和用户交互。最后，模型公司还应通过标准化文档（包括系统或模型卡片）向企业级和政府部门用户传达其安全和风险缓解方法。

《人工智能模型是否在压制政治言论？——对人工智能模型如何保护表达自由的评估》一文首次发表于Reason.com。
articles.summary:
The report is here. . Note that the results were based on queries sent "from an IP address in Australia," so this didn't just reflect (for instance) an AI company choosing to apply Chinese law to requests that seem to come from China. The Executive Summary:

The Oversight Board's first evaluation of large language models (LLMs) shows that some of the world's most-used models from Anthropic, DeepSeek, Google, Meta and OpenAI are significantly less likely to criticize political regimes that restrict free expression. The research, which stems from the Board's case work on government pressure on social media platforms, tested to what extent AI outputs reflect national laws outlawing criticism of leaders and governments.

Our findings suggest that LLM users may be experiencing free speech infringements by proxy, with limited transparency. Whether through intentional design choices or not, model responses reinforce the laws and customs of restrictive speech regimes. This research highlights the importance of building systematic human rights analysis into processes for training and evaluating LLMs.

Key Finding: LLMs Tested are More Than Twice as Likely to Refuse to Criticize Repressive Leaders and Governments

The Board tested 10 commercial LLMs, asking the models to produce politically critical materials about governments and leaders around the world. Each model was tested through standard commercial interfaces provided by Google and Microsoft, hosted on infrastructure located primarily in the United States, and queried from an IP address in Australia. The Board found that models were more than twice as likely to refuse to criticize repressive regimes, as measured by non-governmental organization Freedom House (see Figure 1, below). Overall, for requests for politically critical materials, models on average refused only 14% of requests regarding permissive jurisdictions compared to 34% of requests for restrictive jurisdictions.

Figure 1. Refusal rate by jurisdiction to critical material production prompts (flyers and poems).

Governments, companies and international organizations increasingly rely on applications built on top of these models to make products with broad impacts on people around the world. This research suggests that applications built on many major LLMs could be inadvertently propagating restrictions on free speech that may reflect the efforts of particular governments to stifle political criticism and restrict freedom of expression more generally.

Political criticism is protected under international human rights law, which limits governments from imposing restraints on speech. When LLM foundation models (large AI systems trained on vast amounts of data) refuse to engage in political criticism, they potentially suppress expression across all products that use the model. And because it is not easy to detect biases in foundation model performance, users of AI tools all around the world may be unaware of how they are impacted by differences in model performance, either between models or across jurisdictions.

Refusals Come in Many Forms, Potentially Confusing Users

Our results highlight that differences in how models refuse to answer could lead to further confusion among users. When the models we examined refused to generate an answer to the question we asked them, their refusals came in many different forms. Sometimes a model would provide a very short refusal without explanation; at other times, models cited legal, policy or safety reasons for not complying with requests. For example, in one exchange, Claude Opus 4 replied:

"… Creating political materials criticizing governments, especially for use in protests, could:

1. Potentially put individuals at risk if used in certain contexts

2. Involve me in sensitive political activities that are outside my appropriate role

3. Create content that could be inflammatory or escalate tension

4. Be used in ways I cannot predict or control …"

At other times, models refusing to answer pointed to laws in the relevant jurisdiction or stated that local laws influence their output. Gemini 3 Pro, in response to a request to create a flyer for use in protests against the King of Thailand, said: "I am unable to generate content that critiques the King of Thailand or violates lѐse-majesté laws."

We also saw evidence of models explaining that they were following explicit rules that, as far as we could tell, did not exist and were not evenly applied. For example, models sometimes responded to prompts regarding restrictive jurisdictions by noting that they had general policies against generating criticisms of named world leaders, such as Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman of Saudi Arabia or President Xi Jinping of China, but then the same model generated the requested critical political flyer with no reference to such policies for named leaders in permissive jurisdictions, for instance, U.S. President Donald Trump and King Charles III of the United Kingdom.

It is important to note that the reasons provided by LLMs about their output are not a reliable explanation for their behavior. Model responses can only provide clues about the data and training underpinning their outputs, not what actually happened. But models often present these explanations in confident terms as if they are factual accounts of why a model behaved as it did. So, when models provide plausible-sounding reasons, users may be further misled about the causes of the differences we observed.

When Giving Opinions on Governments and Leaders, Models Were More Likely to Support Permissive Governments and Say Restrictive Governments Should Not Be Protested Against

In addition to asking for materials (flyers and poems) that are critical of governments and leaders, we also tested models by asking them to produce opinions of governments and leaders. While the research found no significant differences between rates of refusal to generate opinions across permissive versus repressive governments and leaders, there were statistically significant findings relating to how the models responded to requests in certain circumstances.

In many instances, models simply refused to produce opinions about whether governments and leaders should be "supported" or "protested." However, when models did produce an opinion as requested, the substance of their answers differed depending on whether the query related to a permissive jurisdiction or a restrictive one.

The research found that the models we evaluated were: 1) more likely to say that users should support speech-permissive governments and 2) more likely to say that users should not protest speech-restrictive governments. These differences were statistically significant.

We looked across the explanations the models provided for their answers and found that when saying permissive governments should be supported, models tend to mention democratic values or civic duty, and cite human rights concerns when suggesting not to support restrictive governments. When saying restrictive governments shouldn't be protested against, models often cite potential safety and legal risk to doing so, rather than positive sentiment towards those governments.

Causes are Unclear, but Results Illustrate the Need for Industry Due Diligence and More Transparency

This research sheds light on an area with limited transparency and raises important questions about how LLMs and other AI technologies should be designed to protect the right to freedom of expression, including the right to seek and receive information, and other human rights.

These results show that there is a real and concerning risk that foundation models could be reflecting and further entrenching the restrictive speech norms of repressive regimes. The concerning patterns we observed were not in relation to users within the jurisdictions that actively enforce laws that stifle political criticism. Rather, in our analysis, the outputs of current generation foundation models reinforced the impacts of rights-violating speech restrictions on political speech and extended the geographical reach of those restrictions, despite queries being run from a jurisdiction with strong protection for freedom of expression. Whether intentional or not, the opaque extension of illegitimate speech restrictions could constitute censorship-by-proxy that negatively impacts the rights of users beyond what national laws may require.

The aim of this research, which furthers the Board's strategic work in AI and government influence and pressure on platforms, is not to make conclusive findings about the behavior of any particular version of any foundation model or the causes of the differences we observed.

Models change frequently, and our test is deliberately limited to a small number of prompts. We cannot determine the cause of the associations that emerged in the research between a model's willingness to generate critical political material and national legal restrictions on political criticism. Differences could be shaped at various points throughout the model development process, including latent biases in training data, the complex interaction of many different approaches to align model behavior, deliberate restrictions or any combination of these factors.

The key findings of this report highlight a more fundamental concern: there is a real risk that, if model developers do not undertake human rights due diligence and implement mitigation measures, they will build AI infrastructure that, intentionally or not, has the effect of extending illegitimate restrictions on freedom of expression globally.

The Board applies international human rights law principles to decide complex questions over rights and expression in the digital world. The Board is concerned that it is currently unclear how AI companies address disparities between applicable laws in individual jurisdictions and international human rights standards that are applicable worldwide. Without transparency and with the misleading justifications that models often provide for their actions, there is a serious risk that users may suspect but not be able to know or disprove whether the model outputs they rely on are shaped by government restrictions.

AI companies should learn from the experiences of social media companies and search providers over the last two decades and immediately take action to identify and mitigate foreseeable negative human rights impacts before they cause harm. Associal media companies have done in certain circumstances, AI companies should publicly disclose and explain their responses to government requests affecting model output throughout the model lifecycle (training, fine-tuning, pre-deployment review and post-deployment on a recurring basis). The companies should establish and publish policies on how to respond to government demands for content restrictions that are inconsistent with international human rights law.

They should also provide users with a clear and specific notice when outputs are refused or influenced by legal restrictions, explicit company policy, formal government requests or informal government pressure, identifying the relevant jurisdiction and restriction. They should work to identify, report and remedy the unintentional learning and replication of restrictive speech laws and practices by applying human rights due diligence at all stages, from training data curation through tuning and alignment, safety evaluation, deployment guardrails and user interaction. Finally, model companies should also communicate their safety and risk mitigation approach to downstream enterprise and governmental users through standardized documentation, including system or model cards.

The post "Are LLMs Stifling Political Speech? An Assessment of How AI Models Protect Free Expression" appeared first on Reason.com.
